title: G4: The normalization on CP^{2k} forces Q(x)=sqrt(x)/tanh(sqrt(x))

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Claim to prove.** Among all multiplicative sequences (power series $Q(x)=1+a_1x+\cdots$), the unique one whose genus equals $1$ on every $\mathbb{CP}^{2k}$ ($k\ge1$) is $Q(x)=\sqrt{x}/\tanh\sqrt{x}$, equivalently $f(z)=z\coth z=\sum_{n\ge0}\frac{2^{2n}B_{2n}}{(2n)!}z^{2n}$.

**Required argument.** On $\mathbb{CP}^{2k}$ the total Pontryagin class is $(1+x^2)^{2k+1}$ ($x\in H^2$ the hyperplane class), so the $L$-number is the coefficient of $x^{2k}$ in $Q(x^2)^{2k+1}$, i.e. $\langle L,[\mathbb{CP}^{2k}]\rangle=[\,x^{2k}\,]\,Q(x^2)^{2k+1}$. Package these into a generating function in $t$ and use **Lagrange inversion** (or a contour-integral/residue computation with substitution $u=\tanh z$) to show the condition $\langle L,[\mathbb{CP}^{2k}]\rangle=1\ \forall k$ is equivalent to $Q(x)=\sqrt{x}/\tanh\sqrt{x}$.

**Verification done by critic (numeric):** with $Q=1+x/3-x^2/45+2x^3/945-\cdots$, the coefficient extraction gives $1$ for $k=1,2,3$. This sub-lemma is fully closable; it just needs to be written out in full (Lagrange inversion step explicit).
